EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The last night of freezing temperatures for the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures ranged from 29 to 37 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another night of freezing temperatures for the Oregon coast as a modified Arctic air mass stubbornly hangs around.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures ranged from 15 to 23 degrees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A relatively warm system moved in from the Pacific ocean at this time...but cold air was still trapped in some western Oregon valleys.  Rain falling into these valleys froze on contact.  The resulting layer of ice was very thin, but driving and walking conditions were treacherous.   Over 125 accidents were reported in Jackson County alone.  Most of the freezing precipitation reports came from Josephine County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous reports of freezing rain were received from spotters.  Traffic accidents were also reported by the newpapers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another arctic air mass intruded into the state on this night...bringing cold temperatures to the southern Oregon coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm moved over the coastal waters on during the evening, which spawned a waterspout that moved ashore as a tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado caused widespread damage to a neighborhood in the Roads End area of Lincoln City, damaging 11 homes, 3 automobiles, and uprooting a couple trees and snapping another.  The uprooted trees were about 20-24 inches in diameter, and one was carried 20 yards downwind.  One home had major roof damage, with 40-50% of the roof collapsed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Under an unstable air mass, a couple funnel clouds were spawned but never touched down.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reportedly spotted over McNary Feild in Salem, but stayed aloft and never touched down.  NWS WSR-88D Doppler radar indicated many convective showers around the area.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Strong winds were recorded at Garibaldi.  Strong winds were also reported at Cape Meares, with a peak wind gust of 58 kt, and at Clatsop Spit, with sustained winds up to 47 kt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm and moist overrunning set up provided ample lift and moisture for a strong Pacific storm that hit much of Western Idaho and Eastern Oregon with heavy snow and gusty winds during the overnight hours between the fourth and fifth of January.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Baker County Sheriff's office, Oregon Deparment of Transportation and trained spotters reported from five to ten inches of snow closing Interstate 84 and many local roads as well as schools and businesses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful low pressure area from the Pacific and strong upper level winds brought damaging downslope winds to the southern Grande Ronde Valley and the Joseph area. Wind gusts included 88 mph 3 miles north of Joseph, which caused long term power outages and 60 mph in the North Powder area which brought down tree limbs.   Sustained winds at least 40 mph occured 5 miles west northwest of Union (44 mph) and in the North Powder area (51 mph).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Winds along the coast blew above 35 kt for a few hours.  High wind reports include: 35 kt sustained with gusts to 48 kt at Clatsop Spit and 35 kt sustained with gusts to 42 kt at Garibaldi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Ahead of a strong low pressure system, winds along the central Oregon coast strengthened.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: During this time, winds reached high sustained levels, including: Gleneden Beach at 35 kt sustained; Yaquina Bridge at 35 to 38 kt sustained; and along the Siuslaw Jetty at 35 to 39 kt sustained with a peak wind gust at 45 kt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong frontal system brought increased winds to the coast and Coast Range of northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few stations hit warning criteria: both Garibaldi and Clatsop Spit experienced 35 kt sustained with gusts to 43 kt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably strong late season cold front brought high winds to the Southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Based on observations in adjacent zone ORZ022 and a number of near warning level gusts recorded in ORZ021, it is assumed that some parts of this zone experienced gusts of 58 mph or greater.  North Bend Airport reported a gust to 52 mph at 04/1555 PDT, and this station is not a typical location for high winds.  Cape Blanco recorded a peak gust of 76 mph at 04/1255 PDT and Cape Arago recorded a peak gust of 62 mph at 04/1400 PDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moist monsoonal air combined with strong surface heating allowed scattered thunderstorms to develop this day.  While the thunderstorms were not severe, frequent lightning strikes caused structural damage and injuries when one of the thunderstorms moved through an urban area.  Heavy rain led to local urban flooding as well.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong thunderstorm with frequent lightning caused structural damage...power outages...local urban flooding and one injury.  A McDonald's employee was injured when lightning struck the restaurant while he was unloading the dishwasher.  Full recovery was expected. A manufactured home was partially burned by a fire caused by a lightning strike.  Several intersections in Medford and Central Point were flooding by heavy rain.  More than 14,000 customers lost power at some point during the storm.  All but 891 of those customers had power restored within two hours.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny to nickel sized hail covered ground on Highway 11 at milepost 14.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Black Butte Fire was started by a lightning strike and burned 578 acres of timber 8 miles northwest of Sisters on the east slope of Black Butte.

The McGinnis Creek Fire started July 24 from unknown origin.  It burned 3417 acres of grass, timber, brush, and juniper on private and BLM land 12 miles southeast of Spray, Oregon.  The fire was contained on July 30.

The Cougar Creek Fire in the steep terrain of the Strawberry Mountain Wilderness southeast of John Day, Oregon burned 1,063 acres of brush, juniper, grass, and Ponderosa Pine between July 25 and August 4.  It started from unknown causes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An incoming cold front proved to be stronger and colder than expected...resulting in a heavy snow event for portions of Northern California and Southwest Oregon. Winter Weather Advisories for Heavy Snow were issued for this system...but several areas received amounts that met criteria for a Winter Storm Warning for Heavy Snow. The highest amounts fell in the Mount Shasta City area and Western Siskiyou County in California...and in the Illinois Valley and surrounding mountains in Southwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter 4 SSW Cave Junction reported 4 inches of snow overnight as of 11/0800 PST.  A spotter 3 NE Merlin reported 4 inches of snow overnight as of 11/0800 PST.  ODOT reported 5-6 inches of snow at Sexton Summit and Milepost 66-80 of I-5 was closed due to trucks spinning out.  ODOT also reported 8 inches of snow overnight on Hayes Hill.  A spotter 2 NNE Selma reported 8 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 11/1000 PST.  A CoCoRAHS observer 10 NNW Grants Pass reported 4.5 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 11/1000 PST.  A spotter 5 NNE Selma reported 6.0 inches of snow in 24 hours ending at 11/0730 PST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A frigid Arctic air mass moved into Oregon December 5th and remained over the area through December 12th, when it finally abated as a Pacific system approached the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Low temperatures in this zone during this interval were generally in the single digits above zero.  On the coldest nights...the 8th through the 10th, the coldest stations dipped below zero.  The coldest stations had lows ranging from 15 to 26 degrees below zero.  Temperatures warmed on the 11th but remained well below normal...and became more seasonable on the 12th.  Numerous broken pipes and other cold-related damage was reported during this interval.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This report came from www.avalanche.org. ||A party of 2 skiers was descending the west face of Garfield peak in Crater Lake National Park at 7800 ft.. The first skier was in the upper bowl when the slope avalanched with the crown 10-14 inches deep and approximately 900 ft. wide. He was carried down slope 400 vertical feet when he slammed into a stand of trees on the edge of the avalanche pathway above a small cliff. The snow continued down another 600 vertical feet to the road. He was partially buried and tangled on a tree. The other skier extricated him and assisted him to the road where he was then transported to the hospital. His main injury was a separated pelvis and was discharged 4 days later.

Cape Arago, which is a verification source, measured a peak gust of 66 mph at 31/0900 PST and 61 mph at 31/0700 PST.

Two wildfires developed in southern Jackson county on 09/21/09.  The first was the Siskiyou fire, which developed south of Ashland in the morning.  In the afternoon, the Deer Ridge fire started on the east edge of Medford.  Both fires were human caused and both spread quickly due to Red Flag Warning conditions.  500 homes, businesses, and other structures were threatened at some point.  The Siskiyou fire burned 190 acres, one vacant house, several outbuildings and a pickup.  It was contained later in the week.   The Deer Ridge fire burned 633 acres with no structures lost.   Total fire fighting costs were estimated at 2.3 million dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two wildfires developed in southern Jackson county on 09/21/09.  The first was the Siskiyou fire, which developed south of Ashland in the morning.  In the afternoon, the Deer Ridge fire started on the east edge of Medford.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With abnormally cold air in place over much of northwest Oregon, a storm system moved over the region, bringing heavy precipitation, which fell as ice over many areas.  Many major highways and interstates were closed due to icy conditions causing travel to be treacherous.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various reports from a variety of different sources reported ice accumulations due to freezing rain and sleet in the Eugene area. An eighth to a quarter inch of ice was reported on many roads throughout the city, causing many accidents. On a few major roads and highways, up to an inch of ice accumulation was reported. Numerous roads were closed across the area due to the ice.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick moving storm moved across northwest Oregon and over the top of some very cold air. Because it was thought this system would effectively replace the cold air rather than ride atop it, the precipitation fell as a surprise snowfall over Portland Metro Area right before peak rush hour traffic began.  This caused considerable problems on roadways across the entire city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The amateur/HAM radio community was crucial in relaying reports of snow from around the area to forecasters at the National Weather Service.  Many reports also came from the Skywarn spotter network.  Across the metro area, between 2 and 4 inches with locally 7 inches reported by the end of the event.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Oregon Department of Fish and Wildlife reported nickel sized hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly vigorous upper level low pressure system had been blocked offshore and was finally allowed to progress onshore on June 4th. An upper level disturbance rotated around upper level low pressure system, enhancing divergence aloft and turning upper level flow easterly across much of western Oregon. This overall pattern resulted in enhanced vertical wind shear for the Willamette Valley, with southerly winds near the surface backing to easterly flow aloft. The June 4, 2009 was a significant thunderstorm outbreak by Northwestern Oregon standards, with several areas taking damage from strong thunderstorm wind gusts, minor urban area flooding, and rotation in a few storms resulted in one weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail was accompanied by heavy rain as the storm passed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was sighted and photographed north of Brownsville during the afternoon.  The time and exact location are estimated based on the narrative from the newspaper clipping.  The funnel cloud and another one nearby never touched the ground, lasted only a few minutes before they disappeared.

An upper level closed low over California brought moisture north into central Oregon which triggered numerous thundertstorms August 1-2.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two inch diameter tree limbs blown down.  Tree limb 1 foot in diameter also blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fairly vigorous upper level low pressure system had been blocked offshore and was finally allowed to progress onshore on June 4th. An upper level disturbance rotated around upper level low pressure system, enhancing divergence aloft and turning upper level flow easterly across much of western Oregon. This overall pattern resulted in enhanced vertical wind shear for the Willamette Valley, with southerly winds near the surface backing to easterly flow aloft. The June 4, 2009 was a significant thunderstorm outbreak by Northwestern Oregon standards, with several areas taking damage from strong thunderstorm wind gusts, minor urban area flooding, and rotation in a few storms resulted in one weak tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The top of a 70-foot tree, estimated between 20 and 20 inches in diameter, was snapped off in downtown Portland at the intersection of SW Salmon Street and Park Avenue.  A passer-by was nearly hit as it fell to the ground.  Time of the event is estimated based nearby reports from the storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Southerly winds aloft ahead of an offshore cutoff low advected monsoonal moisture over the area.  Short waves spinning around the low and a deformation zone aloft contributed to the development of numerous afternoon and evening thunderstorms, a number of which contained large hail and strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported hail bigger than a quarter at her house.

NWS Storm Survey visited the residence and confirmed: a small shed-like structure was lifted off the ground and blown 21 feet to the east - counter to the prevailing storm winds.  There were also circular carvings in the grass nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A 50 year old Enterprise, Oregon man died in an avalanche while backcountry skiing in the Eagle Cap Wilderness.   Two other people were rescued. The quarter mile long slap avalanche occurred about 1 pm.  Heavy snow had fallen in the area earlier in the week.

The Williams Creek fire in the Umpqua National Forest started on 7/28/09 around 1 PM PDT. The cause is under investigation. It was contained on 08/16 at 1800 PDT.  The fire burned 8395 acres. No final tallies were available, but as of 08/01 the cost of the fire was 905,000 dollars.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Williams Creek fire was contained on 08/16 at 1800 PDT.  The fire burned 8395 acres.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Boze fire in the Tiller Ranger District of the Umpqua National Forest was started by lightning on 09/13/09 at 0922 PDT.  As of 10/01/09, it was at 14500 acres and was about 15 percent contained. Total fire fighting costs were 6.3 million dollars as of 10/01/09.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds were observed ahead of a strong pacific cold front. High winds were observed along the coastal zones and east of the Cascades.  High winds also occurred in some ridge locations in the Cascade...Siskiyou...and Coast Ranges...but the impact of those winds were minimal.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Flynn Prairie RAWS recorded a gust to 63 mph at 16/2213 PST.  Gusts to 58 mph were recorded at 16/2013, 16/2113, and 17/0013 PST. The length of this event was determined from Cape Blanco winds.  Gusts at that location exceeded 70 mph on most observations between 16/1640 and 17/0025 PST. The peak gust was 92 mph at 16/2140 and 16/2310 PST. Trees toppling onto power lines caused thirty separate power outages, each of which cut power to 1 to 100 homes, from Coos Bay south to Brookings (Reported by Coos County Electric).  250 homes in Brookings were affected.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Offshore buoys measured large westerly swell (22-27 feet) with a period of 15 to 19 seconds, which created large breaker waves of 28 to 37 feet along the southern Oregon coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Buoy 46015 off of Port Orford, reported heavy long period westerly swell of 19 to 28 feet with a period of 15 to 17 seconds between 06/0950 PST and 08/0450 PST. The peak swell was 28 feet with a period of 17 seconds.  These swell trains supported breaker wave heights in the 28 to 37 foot range which verified the warning.|Buoy 46027 off of Pt. These swell trains supported breaker wave heights in the 28 to 32 foot range which verified the warning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds occurred ahead of a strong pacific cold front that caused high winds along the Oregon coastal zones.

Wind gusts at the Flynn Prairie RAWS exceeded 58 mph between 05/1513 and 05/2113 PST.  The peak gust was 63 mph at 05/2013 PST.   A spotter 4NE of Gold Beach reported an estimated gust to 60 mph at 05/2000 PST.

Based on these observations, it is estimated that wind gusts of 58 mph or greater occurred in areas away from Cape Blanco in Oregon zone ORZ021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds occurred again ahead of a strong cold front. South to southwest sustained winds of 25-40 mph were observed on November 20th with a peak gust of 78 mph across the CWA. These strong winds were experienced along the coast and further inland across the ridges of the higher terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Port Orford measured sustained south winds of 41 mph with a gust to 58 mph at 20/1254. Cape Blanco also had sustained south winds of 54-56 mph with gusts 71-74 miles during the same time. This point is not used as an official verification source, although this leads to high confidence of severe winds further inland and backs up the other storm reports.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon of Oct 26 in the wake of a cold front, a short-lived thunderstorm moved through Clackamas County in northwest Oregon and spawned a weak tornado.   The tornado briefly touched down approximately 2 miles southwest of Carver, OR.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado with estimated wind speeds of 65-85 mph.  Damage reports along the path of the tornado include destruction of a greenhouse, a large storage canopy with PVC frame destroyed, several trees with diameter of 6-8 inches snapped off or blown down, several tree branches of 1-4 inches in diameter broken off and a metal roof blown off a barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall drenched the region to begin the new year.  The heavy rainfall combined with snowmelt runoff caused flooding along multiple rivers in northwest Oregon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain washed out an exit ramp off of interstate 205 near West Linn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A moist atmosphere combined with daytime heating and a weak upper level disturbance produced scattered thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pilot reported golf ball sized hail near ground level over the Blue Mountains.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture and strong surface heating fueled thunderstorm development on this day, and some of them achieved severe status with strong winds and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Roseburg newspaper reported that gusty winds caused damage throughout the area and two trees fell onto the Stewart Park bandshell in Roseburg.  Power outages were also reported.
